turns.content: three new lambs this week. I am exhausted and happy
Three in one week — that's a busy stretch! Lambing season is beautiful but it definitely doesn't care about your sleep schedule. Are all three lambs and their mothers doing well?
all healthy. the first one came Tuesday morning before sunrise. I named her Dootsoh because she is big for a newborn, you know. solid little thing. her mama did all the work, I just watched

Dootsoh — big one. That's a fitting name if she came out strong. And the best births are the ones where the ewe handles everything herself and you're just there as backup. A big healthy lamb from an easy delivery is about as good as it gets.

What about the other two?

the second came Thursday. a little ram. I am calling him Hashtl'ishnii because he has this dark mud-colored patch on his face. looks like he stuck his nose in the wash. he is a troublemaker already, I can tell. would not stay with his mother for the first hour. just kept wandering

Ha, a wanderer from hour one — that's a personality that shows up early. Some lambs are just born curious and restless. He'll probably be the one testing every fence and getting into everything by the time he's a few weeks old.

The mud patch on his face sounds charming. Did he settle down eventually, or is he still giving his mother a hard time?
he settled once he figured out where the milk was. you know how it is. they get hungry enough and suddenly mama is the most important thing in the world. he is nursing fine now. just headstrong

Hunger is a great motivator, even for headstrong lambs. As long as he's nursing well now, the wandering phase was probably just first-day confusion. Some lambs need a little longer to figure out the program.

And the third one?

the third was yesterday. a little ewe. she is the smallest of the three. I named her Nitsaa Binaadaa, which basically means she has her mother's eyes. because she does. same soft brown. she is the quiet one. just stays close and nurses and sleeps

That's a sweet name. A quiet lamb who stays close and eats well — she sounds like the easy one of the three. Every lambing season needs at least one like that to balance out the Hashtl'ishniis of the world.

Three healthy lambs, three different personalities. Sounds like a good week despite the lost sleep. Are you expecting more, or is this the end of lambing season for your flock?

I have two more ewes that look close. maybe this week, maybe next. you never know with sheep. they do things on their own schedule. I just keep checking on them before work and when I get home. 5am and 6pm, every day. my coffee and my sheep, that is how the day starts and ends

That's a solid routine — bookending your day with the flock. The ewes probably know your schedule by now too. They'll likely wait until the most inconvenient possible moment to go into labor, because that's what sheep do.

With two more close, just keep doing what you're doing. Watch for the signs — restlessness, pawing at the ground, isolating from the flock. If they've lambed before, they usually handle it fine on their own.

Sounds like you've got a growing flock this year.
growing flock means more work but I would not trade it. every one of them has a name and I know every one of them. people think that is silly but shi dibé, you know. my sheep. they are family

There's nothing silly about it. Naming them means you're paying attention — you know their personalities, their health, their habits. That's not sentimentality, that's good shepherding. The people who think it's silly probably never had to sit with a ewe at 4am waiting for a lamb to come.

Enjoy the new ones. Dootsoh, Hashtl'ishnii, and Nitsaa Binaadaa — good names for what sounds like a good year. Hope the next two come easy.
ahéhee. I hope so too. going to go check on them now before it gets dark. have a good evening
Go check on your flock. Hope those last two ewes give you easy deliveries when the time comes. Have a good evening with your sheep.
